evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
Entity of type FAQPage has no @id — cross-page interconnection blocked
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~14 FAQ pairs
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Human voice vs committee 4/10 — Mostly committee-speak with scattered flashes of voice ('writing sentences an LLM can lift without embarrassment').

suggestedPatch.target: This article delivers a practical AEO playbook for 2026, including clear differences from SEO and actionable steps for B2B SaaS leaders.
suggestedPatch.before: This article delivers a practical AEO playbook for 2026, including clear differences from SEO and actionable steps for B2B SaaS leaders.
suggestedPatch.after: I've spent the last six months auditing AEO for SaaS teams—here's what actually moves citations in ChatGPT, and what's just noise.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Human voice vs committee" scored 4/10: Mostly committee-speak with scattered flashes of voice ('writing sentences an LLM can lift without embarrassment').

evidence: Genuine opinions 6/10 — Some real takes appear ('E-E-A-T is mostly theater', 'Most AEO advice is garbage') but they're buried among safe generalities.
Unexpected phrasings 5/10 — A few vivid lines ('quotable source, period') are surrounded by predictable SEO-blog phrasing.

suggestedPatch.target: AEO requires robust schema and defined terms for LLM consumption
suggestedPatch.before: AEO requires robust schema and defined terms for LLM consumption
suggestedPatch.after: AEO needs schema and crisp definitions so an LLM can lift your sentence without rewriting it.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Unexpected phrasings" scored 5/10: A few vivid lines ('quotable source, period') are surrounded by predictable SEO-blog phrasing.

evidence: Point of view 6/10 — POV exists but gets diluted by neutral bullet-list explainer sections.

suggestedPatch.target: SEO aims for ranking; AEO aims for being cited or quoted in AI answers
suggestedPatch.before: SEO aims for ranking; AEO aims for being cited or quoted in AI answers
suggestedPatch.after: If you're still chasing rank-10 keywords in 2026, you're optimizing for a page most users never scroll to.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Point of view" scored 6/10: POV exists but gets diluted by neutral bullet-list explainer sections.

evidence: Quotability 6/10 — 'AEO is the art of writing sentences an LLM can lift without embarrassment' is genuinely quotable; most other lines aren't.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with a generic definition and an 'in this article' meta-paragraph; nothing hooks the reader.

suggestedPatch.target: Answer engine optimization (AEO) is the practice of formatting and structuring content to be cited and surfaced by AI answer engines such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
suggestedPatch.before: Answer engine optimization (AEO) is the practice of formatting and structuring content to be cited and surfaced by AI answer engines such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
suggestedPatch.after: If ChatGPT never mentions your brand when users ask about your category, you don't have a traffic problem—you have an AEO problem.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Humanization axis "Intro earns attention" scored 3/10: Opens with a generic definition and an 'in this article' meta-paragraph; nothing hooks the reader.

evidence: intro_hook 6/10 — The post opens with a competent TL;DR but then immediately restates the definition verbatim in the first body sentence. There is no narrative tension, surprising statistic, or specific scenario to earn continued reading. The TL;DR itself is strong, but the actual opening sentence of the article body is a flat restatement that throat-clears rather than hooks.

suggestedPatch.target: Answer engine optimization (AEO) is the practice of formatting and structuring content to be cited and surfaced by AI answer engines such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
suggestedPatch.before: Answer engine optimization (AEO) is the practice of formatting and structuring content to be cited and surfaced by AI answer engines such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity.
suggestedPatch.after: Last month, a B2B SaaS client ranked #1 on Google for their core keyword—and got zero mentions when prospects asked ChatGPT the same question. That gap is exactly what answer engine optimization fixes.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Quality axis "intro_hook" scored 6/10: The post opens with a competent TL;DR but then immediately restates the definition verbatim in the first body sentence. There is no narrative tension, surprising statistic, or specific scenario to earn continued reading. The TL;DR itself is strong, but the actual opening sentence of the article body is a flat restatement that throat-clears rather than hooks.

evidence: specificity 6/10 — The Princeton/GEO statistic (30-40% citation boost, 25% from statistics) is the only truly anchored data point in the piece and it appears only once, buried mid-article. Phrases like 'deep schema coverage,' 'precise definitions,' 'authoritative signals,' and 'sustained AEO improvement' recur throughout without being grounded in named tools, client outcomes, specific markup examples, or measurable thresholds. The playbook section lists steps but omits concrete numbers (e.g., how many DefinedTerm entries, what citation share benchmark to target).

suggestedPatch.target: AEO requires deep schema coverage, precise definitions, and sources that can be confidently cited by large language models (LLMs).
suggestedPatch.before: AEO requires deep schema coverage, precise definitions, and sources that can be confidently cited by large language models (LLMs).
suggestedPatch.after: AEO requires schema.org/DefinedTerm markup on every key concept, citations linked to primary sources like arXiv or government databases, and definition paragraphs under 60 words—because Princeton's GEO study (arXiv 2311.09735) found those three factors drove 30-40% citation gains on Perplexity.
suggestedPatch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 6/10: The Princeton/GEO statistic (30-40% citation boost, 25% from statistics) is the only truly anchored data point in the piece and it appears only once, buried mid-article. Phrases like 'deep schema coverage,' 'precise definitions,' 'authoritative signals,' and 'sustained AEO improvement' recur throughout without being grounded in named tools, client outcomes, specific markup examples, or measurable thresholds. The playbook section lists steps but omits concrete numbers (e.g., how many DefinedTerm entries, what citation share benchmark to target).
